steps.title: Set up the equation for analysis
steps.content:
The equation $\sqrt{k - x} = 32 - x$ has constraints: the domain requires $k - x \geq 0$ and $32 - x \geq 0$, so $x \leq \min(k, 32)$. To find when there is exactly one solution, square both sides.

$$k - x = (32 - x)^2$$
$$k - x = 1024 - 64x + x^2$$
$$x^2 - 63x + (1024 - k) = 0$$

A solution to the original equation must be a root of this quadratic that also satisfies the domain constraint $x \leq \min(k, 32)$.

steps.title: Check the discriminant for one repeated root
steps.content:
For the original equation to have exactly one real solution, one key scenario is when the squared quadratic has exactly one root (a double root). Compute the discriminant:

$$\Delta = 63^2 - 4(1024 - k) = 3969 - 4096 + 4k = 4k - 127$$

Setting $\Delta = 0$ gives $4k - 127 = 0$, so $4k = 127$. The repeated root is:

$$x = \frac{63}{2} = 31.5$$

steps.title: Verify the root is valid
steps.content:
At $4k = 127$, we have $k = 31.75$. The domain is $x \leq \min(31.75, 32) = 31.75$. Since \$31.5 < 31.75$, the root $x = 31.5$ is within the domain.

Substitute into the original equation to confirm:
$$\sqrt{31.75 - 31.5} = 32 - 31.5$$
$$\sqrt{0.25} = 0.5$$
$$0.5 = 0.5 \,\checkmark$$

This is exactly one solution.

steps.title: Confirm this is the minimum
steps.content:
Check neighboring cases to ensure $4k = 127$ is minimal:

• If $4k < 127$: the discriminant is negative, so no real roots exist—no solutions to the original equation.

• If $127 < 4k \leq 128$: the discriminant is positive, giving two distinct roots. Both fall within the domain $x \leq k$ (since $k < 32$), yielding two solutions.

• If $4k > 128$ (i.e., $k > 32$): the discriminant is positive, but the larger root exceeds $x = 32$, leaving only one root in the valid domain $x \leq 32$—one solution.

The minimum value of $4k$ that produces exactly one real solution is $4k = 127$.
